title: Expected Value & Why the House Wins
subtitle: The quiet number behind every casino
realWorldHook: A roulette bet pays 35-to-1 and feels like a jackpot waiting to happen. Over time it bleeds you at a steady, mathematically guaranteed rate.
conceptSummary: Expected value = Σ(outcome × probability). A negative-EV game can feel winnable and still drain you.

steps.title: The jackpot that isn't
steps.body:
A roulette bet on a single number pays [[35-to-1|If you win, you collect 35 dollars for every 1 you bet. It sounds huge — but the number only lands about 1 spin in 38, less often than the payout makes it feel.]] and feels like a jackpot waiting to happen.

But over time it bleeds you at a steady, mathematically guaranteed rate. Casinos, lotteries, and insurers all run on the same quiet number: [[expected value|The average result per play if you repeated the bet forever, each outcome weighted by its chance. A *negative* expected value means a slow, guaranteed drain over time.]].

Learn to compute it and you can see the leak in any bet.

steps.title: What expected value is
steps.body:
Expected value (EV) is the average outcome per play, weighting each result by its probability:

**EV = Σ (outcome × probability)**

Bet $1 on a single roulette number. You win $35 with probability 1/38 and lose $1 with probability 37/38. EV ≈ −$0.05 per dollar. Every spin, on average, hands the house its [[house edge|The small average loss baked into every bet — here about 5 cents per dollar. It's how a casino profits with near-certainty even though any single spin is unpredictable.]].

steps.prompt: You start with $100 and make this negative-EV bet 100 times. Where do you end up?

options.label: Could be up or down short-term, but trending down

steps.revealMessage: Let's play it out and watch the bankroll trajectory.

revealByOption.a: It can happen short-term — variance is real and a big payout might land. But the long-run pull is downward. Let's watch the two fight it out.
revealByOption.b: That's exactly the right mental model — variance up close, a downward trend over time. Let's watch it play out.
revealByOption.c: It almost never lands exactly even — and the drift is downward, not flat. Let's play and see.

steps.prompt: Run 300 gamblers at once. Their bankrolls fan out — a lucky few spike, most bleed — while the bold average line tracks the true EV trend and the histogram below shows where everyone actually ends up. Slide the payout to a fair wheel and watch the crowd recenter on $100.
A handful of the 300 gamblers finished ahead, but the average line slid steadily below $100. What does that tell you?

options.label: Variance can put you ahead short-term, but negative EV wins out over time
You'll win if you just keep playing longer

feedback.correct: Exactly. Being up early is variance — noise. The EV is negative, so the more you play, the more certainly you trend down. The house doesn't need every round, just the average.
feedback.incorrect: Look at the EV line versus your bankroll. The bankroll wiggles around, but the trend follows the negative EV downward.

byOption.b: It isn't fair — a fair game has EV = 0. Here EV is negative, which is precisely why it drifts down.
byOption.c: Playing longer makes it *worse*, not better — more plays means the average converges to the negative EV. That's the casino's edge.

steps.hint: Short-term swings are variance; the long-term direction is set by the sign of the EV.

steps.title: Find the leak
steps.body:
You might be up right now — [[variance|How much individual results swing around the average. High variance is why you can be ahead short-term even in a losing game — but it washes out over many plays.]] does that. But when EV is negative, the more you play, the more certainly you trend down.

The house doesn't need to win every round; it needs the average. Compute the EV of any bet, contract, or gamble and you can see the leak before you step in.

That's the long run — the thread running through all five lessons.
